content:
5 min read
Just now

--

This is the second article in the series “What the Ocean Taught Me About AI.” If you haven’t read the first, the short version: the mathematics I used to model the ocean turns out to be structurally identical to the mathematics powering modern language models. This article goes deeper — into the temporal dimension.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

A buoy in the Atlantic
Consider a measurement buoy, somewhere off the coast of France. It records sea surface temperature and wave height, without stops, every hour, day after day, year after year.
If you plot the data, something immediately strikes you. The temperature is never still. It drifts upward, drifts downward, responds to storms and seasons and passing currents. But it never drifts freely. It always, eventually, comes back.
There is a long-term mean — the climatological average for that location, that time of year. And no matter how far the temperature wanders from it, it is always being pulled back. Not abruptly. Slowly, continuously, with a strength proportional to how far it has strayed.
In a sense, the ocean remembers where it came from. This behaviour has a name in mathematics, mean reversion, often described via a process called Ornstein-Uhlenbeck.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The mathematics of slow forgetting
The Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process describes a system that drifts randomly but is always pulled back toward a mean:
dX = −λ(X − μ)dt + σ dW
In plain language, three forces acting simultaneously:
- −λ(X − μ)dt— the pull toward the mean. The further you drift, the stronger the pull. λ controls how fast you are reeled back in.
- σ dW — random fluctuation. The ocean is never perfectly still. Storms, currents, passing ships of warm water — randomness is always present.
- dX : The balance between them — what you observe is neither pure drift nor pure noise, but something in between. Just a process with memory.
The memory is encoded in the covariance: how correlated is the wave height (or temperature) at time *t* with the wave height at time t + τ? The answer decays exponentially:
Cov(X_t, X_{t+τ}) = (σ²/2λ) · e^{−λ|τ|}
Large λ — short memory. The system forgets quickly. Small λ — long memory. The system carries its past far into the future.
This is the forgetting function of the ocean. It is also, it turns out, the forgetting function of GPT.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The discrete version — and why it matters
This is an AR(1) model — autoregression of order one. Each value is a weighted combination of the previous value and a new random shock. The weight ρ controls memory: ρ close to 1 means long memory, ρ close to 0 means the system nearly forgets everything each step.
It is well known that this discrete autoregression converges exactly to the continuous OU process. The two are the same dynamical system, one continuous, one discretised.
Now read the GPT next-token prediction rule:
P(token_t | token_1, …, token_{t−1})
Each token is predicted from all previous tokens — a discrete autoregression over a sequence. The attention mechanism computes a weighted combination of past representations, where the weights decay with distance — exactly as ρ^k decays with lag k in the AR(1) model.
GPT is a discretized Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process over semantic space. The ocean and the language model are running the same dynamical system. One in temperature, one in meaning.
What λ means for language models
A model with low effective λ — long memory — maintains coherence over hundreds of tokens. It remembers the topic introduced at the beginning, the constraints established in the first paragraph, the name of the character introduced on page one. It is the North Atlantic in winter — slow to change, carrying its history.
A model with high effective λ — short memory — is coherent sentence by sentence but loses the thread over longer passages. Most current language models have a theoretical context window of tens of thousands of tokens. But their *effective* context — the distance over which they maintain genuine semantic coherence — is much shorter. The forgetting function is not flat. It decays. Exponentially.
This is why AI assistants lose track of instructions given early in a long conversation. Not because they are broken. Because their λ is higher than presumed.
The temporal dimension of the ocean, revisited
In the first article, we saw how the spatial structure of the ocean — the horizontal plane of currents, wind and waves — maps onto the spatial structure of attention. This article adds the second dimension: time.
The OU process is the temporal kernel of the ocean model. It governs how the field evolves from one moment to the next. When you compose the OU temporal kernel with the spatial flow map ψ, the covariance becomes non-separable. Space and time cannot be disentangled. What happened upstream yesterday matters for what is happening downstream today.
Language models face the same coupling. The meaning of a word depends not just on its neighbours in the current sentence, but on the history of the conversation — what was said earlier, what context was established, what direction the dialogue has been flowing. Space and time are coupled in language too.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

What it means for AI practitioners
Every language model has an implicit forgetting rate — how fast the influence of early context decays as the conversation grows. Most enterprise deployments ignore this completely. They treat a 10-message conversation the same as a 100-message one. In practice, coherence degrades not because the model is broken but because its effective memory is shorter than its context window suggests. If your AI assistant loses track of instructions mid-conversation, this is the mechanism. The fix for this decay is architectural — retrieval, summarization, or explicit memory injection — not prompt engineering.
What comes next
We have now covered two dimensions — the horizontal plane of spatial dynamics, and the temporal dimension of memory and forgetting. There is a third dimension we have not yet touched.
The ocean is not flat. And it is not just time that adds depth.
Below the surface, the physics changes. Wind governs the top few metres. Currents run through the middle layers. At depth, ancient circulation moves so slowly it carries water that last saw sunlight centuries ago.
The transformer has the same structure. Early layers handle surface patterns — syntax, local context, the wind of language. Deep layers handle something else entirely — abstraction, reasoning, the slow circulation of meaning across the whole sequence.
In the next article, we push the analogy further and go beyond the surface.
